query: structures on the floor of the third ventricle

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Third Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The third ventricle is a median slit-like cavity situated between the 2 thalami and part of the hypothalamus. In the anterosuperior aspect, it communicates with the lateral ventricles while on its posteroinferior aspect it communicates with the fourth ventricle through the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius. The space of the third ventricle is lined by ependyma and is traversed by a mass of grey matter called interthalamic adhesion or Massa intermedia, located posterior to the foramen of Monroe and connects the 2 thalami. It may be absent in about 30% of human brains. It has a roof, a floor, anterior and posterior wall, and 2 lateral walls. The anterior wall is formed from above downward by: Anterior columns of fornix that diverge laterally into the lateral walls. Anterior commissure Lamina terminalis, which is a thin sheet of grey matter that extends from the rostrum of the corpus callosum superiorly to optic chiasma inferiorly. The posterior wall is formed from above downward by:

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Third Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The roof is formed by a sheet of ependyma connecting the upper border of the lateral wall of the ventricle. It is covered by a triangular fold of pia mater called tela choroidea. Two longitudinal vascular fringes hang downward from tela choroidea and form the choroid plexus of the third ventricle. The lateral wall consists of a curved hypothalamic sulcus extending from the interventricular foramen to the cerebral aqueduct. The sulcus divides the lateral wall into 2 parts: Larger upper part: Formed by the medial surface of the anterior two-thirds of the thalamus Smaller lower part: Formed by the hypothalamus and is continuous with the floor. The floor descends ventrally and is formed from before backward by: Optic chiasma Tuber cinereum and infundibulum Mammillary body Posterior perforated substance Tegmentum of midbrain The third ventricle protrudes into the surrounding structure in the form of recesses. There are 5 following recesses:

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The fourth ventricle is a broad, tent-like cavity of the hindbrain filled with CSF. It is bounded anteriorly by the pons and cranial half of medulla and posteriorly by the cerebellum. It appears triangular on the sagittal section and rhomboidal on a horizontal section. Superiorly, it is continuous with the cerebral aqueduct while inferiorly it is continuous with the central canal of the spinal cord. Recesses of the Fourth Ventricle There are 5 recesses:

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Lateral Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral ventricle is a C-shaped cavity situated within each cerebral hemisphere. It is lined by ependyma and filled with CSF. It has a capacity of 7 to 10 ml. The 2 lateral ventricles are separated from each other by a thin vertical sheet of nervous tissue called septum pellucidum covered on either side by ependyma. It communicates with the third ventricle through the interventricular foramen of Monro. Each of the lateral ventricles is made a central part (body) and 3 horns (cornua) namely the anterior horn, posterior horn, and inferior horn. On the coronal section, it appears triangular anteriorly and rectangular posteriorly. Central Part (Body)

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Lateral recess: There are 2 on either side, between the inferior cerebellar peduncle and the peduncle of flocculus dorsally. Anteriorly, it is crossed by the branch of the glossopharyngeal and vagus nerve. Laterally, it extends into the subarachnoid space through an aperture called the foramen of Luschka. Through this foramen, a part of the choroid plexus of the fourth ventricle also protrudes. A medial dorsal recess that extends into the white core of cerebellum Two lateral dorsal recess on either side of median dorsal recess above the inferior medullary velum Boundaries Lateral wall: The fourth ventricle is bounded inferolaterally by gracile and cuneate tubercles and inferior cerebellar peduncles and superolaterally by the superior cerebellar peduncle.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Third Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Infundibular recess: It is a deep recess protruding downwards from the tuber cinereum into the pituitary stalk i.e infundibulum. Optic recess: It is an angular recess situated at the junction of the anterior wall and the floor. Its anterior wall is formed by lamina terminalis and the posteroinferior wall by optic chiasma. Anterior recess (vulva of the ventricle): It is a diverticulum that is bounded anteriorly by the anterior commissure and posteriorly by the diverging columns of the fornix. Pineal recess: It is a small outpouching that extends into the stalk of the pineal body. Surprapineal recess: It is a diverticulum that lies anterosuperior to pineal recess and is lined by ependyma. It normally measures 2 to 3 mm. It is named as the pressure diverticulum of the third ventricle as it becomes distended in the case of hypertensive hydrocephalus. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Third Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Pineal gland Posterior commissure Cerebral aqueduct

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Floor:  The floor is formed by the posterior surface of the pons and upper medulla. It is rhomboid/diamond-shaped, so often called rhomboid fossa. It is divisible into 3 parts: An upper triangular part formed by the posterior surface of the pons. A lower triangular part formed by the posterior surface of the upper medulla. The intermediate part is formed by the base of the upper triangular part above and by a line joining the taenia below. Delicate bundles of transversely arranged fibers are present on the surface called striae medullaris.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: Dorsal wall (Roof): The cephalic portion of the roof is formed by 2 superior cerebellar peduncles whose medial margins overlap the ventricle on reaching the inferior colliculi. A thin sheet of white matter called superior medullary velum bridges the gap between the superior cerebellar peduncle. It is covered dorsally by the lingula of the superior vermis of the cerebellum.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Cerebral Aqueduct (Sylvian) -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The pars anterior is a dorsally based triangle under the posterior commissure that connects to the third ventricle and runs ventrally to the tectum of the midbrain. It is followed by the antrum that is flanked on either side by constrictions formed by the superior and inferior colliculus. Finally, the pars posterior has the narrowest lumen and connects to the rhomboid fossa of the fourth ventricle. [1] [3] [4] The aqueduct is highly variable in its size, but the impression caused by the inferior colliculi creates the smallest diameter in the aqueduct. [2] Phase-contrast magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) can be used to analyze the flow of CSF throughout the ventricular system. The CSF flow velocity increases as it flows from the pars anterior to the smaller diameter pars posterior. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Fourth Ventricle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The lateral wall of the fourth ventricle is surrounded inferolaterally by the cuneate and gracile tubercules, along with the inferior cerebellar peduncles. The lateral wall is also surrounded superolaterally by the superior cerebellar peduncle. The dorsal wall, or roof of the fourth ventricle, is formed by two cerebellar peduncles that are bridged together by a thin sheet of white matter called the superior medullary velum. The floor of the fourth ventricle forms from the posterior surface of the pons and the superior medulla. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The lower half of the roof is covered by a thin sheet of non-nervous tissue, the inferior medullary velum, which is formed by the ventricular ependyma and the tela choroidea of the fourth ventricle.  The lower part of inferior medullary velum has a large midline aperture called the foramen of Magendie through which the fourth ventricle communicates with the subarachnoid space of the cerebellomedullary cistern, i.e., cisterna magna.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Lateral Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The central part lies within the parietal lobe. It extends from the interventricular foramen anteriorly to the splenium of the corpus callosum posteriorly. The roof is formed by the inferior surface of the body of the corpus callosum. The medial wall is formed by the septum pellucidum majorly and the body of the fornix in the lower part. The floor is concave and is formed by the following structures in the lateral to medial order: The body of the caudate nucleus (a small bundle of white fibers) Stria terminalis and thalamostriate vein The lateral part of the superior surface of the thalamus Choroid plexus that invaginate into the lateral ventricle through a slit space between the fornix and upper surface of thalamus, choroid fissure Anterior Horn The anterior horn moves forward and slightly lateral and downward to lie in the frontal lobe, so also referred to as the frontal horn. It has a roof, a floor, anterior and medial wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Fourth Ventricle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The ventricles of the brain are the sites of cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) production. The lateral ventricles are the largest, and most proximal, ventricles in the central nervous system (CNS). CSF produced in the lateral ventricles goes into the interventricular foramen of Monro. The interventricular foramen of Monro connects the lateral ventricles to the third ventricle. The third ventricle connects to the fourth ventricle through the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius. CSF flows through this entire pathway and then exits the fourth ventricle into the surrounding CNS tissue or the central spinal canal. This article will focus on the anatomy, function, and clinical relevance of the fourth ventricle. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Ventricular System -- Structure and Function -- Fourth Ventricle
retrieved_docs.content: The tela choroidea of the fourth ventricle is a double layer fold of pia-mater. The dorsal layer lines the inferior vermis and is reflected upon itself to form the ventral layer on reaching the nodule of the cerebellum. Laterally, it meets the inferolateral border of the ventricular floor marked by a white ridge called Taenia. Inferiorly, the 2 taeniae meet to form a small fold called obex while superiorly it passes along the lateral recess. The 2 layers of tela choroidea enclose the choroid plexus of the fourth ventricle in the form of vascular fringes. The plexus assumes a "T" shape with a vertical and horizontal limb. The vertical limb 2 long fringes in the median plane fused at the cranial end while the horizontal limb continues in the lateral recess through the aperture foramen of Luschka into the subarachnoid space. In certain cases, this foramen is closed by a membrane. This forms a pouch containing choroid plexus at the cerebellopontine angle called Bochdalek's flower basket. Enlargement of this pouch may give rise to clinical symptoms. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Neuroanatomy, Fourth Ventricle -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The fourth ventricle is the most inferiorly located ventricle, draining directly into the central canal of the spinal cord. Superiorly, it connects to the third ventricle through a thin canal called the cerebral aqueduct of Sylvius. It is surrounded anteriorly by the pons and medulla, posteriorly by the cerebellum, and inferiorly by the spinal canal and spinal cord. At its superolateral portions, two channels drain CSF into the surrounding neural tissue. These are called the lateral apertures, or the foramina of Luschka. Inferomedially is the median aperture or the foramen of Magendie. These apertures help spread CSF around the spinal cord and the surrounding neural structures. All of the CSF that does not drain out of these apertures goes into the central canal of the spinal cord.

This type of shunt diverts cerebrospinal fluid from the brain’s ventricles to an extracranial location, typically the peritoneal cavity, and is employed in the management of conditions such as hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2238
retrieved_docs.content: Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a neurosurgical procedure that falls under the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. Within this category, the specific technique known as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is identified as a child procedure, representing a particular method of diverting cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location such as the peritoneal cavity. Recognizing that Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is a subset of the general shunt management interventions and that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a specific variant of this procedure aids clinicians in selecting and performing the appropriate surgical approach for conditions like hydrocephalus.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: The brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway serves as the direct procedural site for a ventricular shunt to nasopharynx. The shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is positioned within the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt and has a child procedure called التحويلة البطينية البريتونية (ventriculoperitoneal shunt). Additionally, the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is related to, and possibly synonymous with, the general procedure of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. These connections clarify the hierarchical classification and anatomical context of various extracranial ventricular shunt procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2518

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is recognized as a neurological disorder that involves the brain and nerves, and it is specifically identified within the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway where excess cerebrospinal fluid accumulates. Clinically, hydrocephalus is associated with the development of dementia (خرف). In the management of hydrocephalus, shunting procedures are commonly employed; the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) frequently co‑occurs with the condition and represents a specific type of shunt that diverts fluid from a cerebral ventricle to an extracranial site. This shunt is a child concept of the broader category “shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is nested under the procedural domain of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. Another variant, the ventricular shunt to the nasopharynx, is performed directly on the brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway and is considered closely related, and possibly synonymous, with the general insertion/replacement/removal procedure for extracranial ventricular shunts. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians diagnose hydrocephalus, anticipate its cognitive complications, and select appropriate shunting interventions for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1087
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt is a neurosurgical procedural category that is classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية. This category also functions as its own classifier, defining the scope of procedures that involve the placement, exchange, or extraction of shunts that divert cerebrospinal fluid from the cerebral ventricles to an extracranial location. Within this broader category, the specific procedure Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is identified as a subordinate (child) entity, indicating that it falls under the umbrella of insertion, replacement, or removal activities. Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site further includes the child entity التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, which denotes a ventriculoperitoneal shunt—a common type of extracranial ventricular shunt used to treat hydrocephalus. By mapping these hierarchical relationships, clinicians can understand that التحويلة البطينية البريتونية is a particular instance of a Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site, and both are encompassed by the broader procedural group of Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt, classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية. This structured knowledge aids in accurate coding, procedural planning, and communication across multidisciplinary teams caring for patients requiring ventricular shunt management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2897
retrieved_docs.content: The central nervous system (centrální nervový systém) is described as a physical component of the dura mater, indicating its anatomical relationship within the meningeal coverings. In clinical assessment, the central nervous system serves as the finding site for central nervous system injury (إصابة الجهاز العصبي المركزي), highlighting its relevance as the location where such pathological findings are identified. Understanding that the central nervous system is both anatomically linked to the dura mater and is the primary site of CNS injuries informs diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in neurology and neurosurgery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 767
retrieved_docs.content: Hydrocephalus (استسقاء الدماغ) is classified within the broader “Brain and Nerves” system and is considered a member of both the Neurological Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) and the Physical & Psychosomatic Disorders (PsycINFO Subcluster Term) categories, indicating its relevance to both neurological and psychosomatic health. In hierarchical terminology, the Czech term “hlava” (head) is also listed as a parent concept for hydrocephalus, reinforcing its location within cranial anatomy. The condition is typically identified in the brain cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) pathway, which serves as the primary finding site for hydrocephalus. This pathway is directly involved in several surgical interventions that redirect CSF flow. One such intervention is the ventricular‑to‑nasopharynx shunt, which is described as a direct procedural site within the brain CSF pathway and is closely related—often synonymous—to the broader category of insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts. A more specific type of extracranial shunt is the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية). This shunt is a child concept of the general “Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site,” which itself is a child of the overarching procedure category “insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt.” Hydrocephalus frequently co‑occurs with the ventricular‑peritoneal shunt, reflecting the common clinical need to treat the excess CSF accumulation with this diversion technique. Overall, hydrocephalus is situated within a network of anatomical, diagnostic, and therapeutic concepts that span neuro‑anatomy, disorder classification, and surgical management, highlighting the importance of integrated care that addresses both the underlying CSF dynamics and the associated procedural interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 2 -- Community 1021
retrieved_docs.content: The procedure known as Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt is classified as تحويلة بطينية وعائية and is performed within the specialty of جراحة الأعصاب. This neurosurgical activity is directed at the Brain cerebrospinal fluid pathway, which serves as the direct_procedure_site_of the ventricular shunt to nasopharynx. The ventricular shunt to nasopharynx is closely related to, and may be synonymous with, the general category of Insertion; replacement; or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt. Within the hierarchical structure of shunt procedures, Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site is the parent entity for specific shunt types such as التحويلة البطينية البريتونية, while itself being a child of the broader insertion, replacement, or removal procedure. The nervous system (nervový systém) is the anatomical system addressed by these interventions, and it includes related procedural sites such as إحصار عصبي عضلي. Together, these relationships delineate how extracranial ventricular shunt procedures are organized, classified, and anatomically targeted within neurosurgical practice.

The nervous system also encompasses procedures such as muscle‑nerve entrapment (إحصار عصبي عضلي) and is closely linked to neurosurgical interventions for CSF diversion. Insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunts—including the intraperitoneal ventricular shunt (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية) and shunts to the nasopharynx—are essential for managing ventricular enlargement or hydrocephalus. Systemic nervous system disorders are broadly related to vascular headache, illustrating how anatomical sites, disease classifications, clinical similarities, and surgical treatments are interwoven in the comprehensive understanding and care of nervous system disorders.

This procedural category is hierarchical, with the general “insertion, replacement, or removal of extracranial ventricular shunt” parent procedure and specific child procedures including ventriculo‑atrial shunts (تحويلة بطينية وعائية), peritoneal ventricular shunts (التحويلة البطينية البريتونية), and nasopharyngeal ventricular shunts. Neuromuscular blockade (إحصاب عصبي عضلي) is another therapeutic intervention linked to the nervous system. Collectively, these entities and relationships illustrate the interconnected anatomy, pathology, diagnostic imaging, and neurosurgical interventions central to the assessment and management of central nervous system disorders.

retrieved_docs.content: A CT scan of the head that demonstrates a periventricular cyst is classified as a brain cyst (كيسة دماغية). This imaging finding indicates the presence of a cystic lesion located near the brain’s ventricular system, and it is recognized within medical terminology as a type of intracranial cyst. Identifying the lesion as a brain cyst guides further diagnostic evaluation and informs appropriate clinical management.
